_metadata.source: Younger's Linear A Lexicon (2024 edition, 10960 lines)
_metadata.methodology: Systematic extraction from Younger lexicon, cross-referenced with glossary suffix entries

reduplication.note: SA reduplication most productive (ja-sa-sa-ra family). Also QA, KI, DI, TE, TI, MA, DA, PA3, RA.

confirmed_compounds.analysis: i-da (Mt. Ida) + ma-te (mother) = Mother of Ida
a-di-ki-te (Dikte) + te + du-pu2-re (master) = master of Dikte

-JA <-> Hurrian relational -ya.note: Both form relational adjectives. Direct functional match.

-NA <-> Hurrian article -na/-ni.note: High frequency consistent with article function.

-TE <-> Hurrian allative -da.note: Both mark location/direction. Regular voicing difference.

-NE <-> Hurrian genitive -(v)e.note: Possible but Hurrian genitive is -(v)e not -ne.

-ME <-> Hurrian possessive.note: Hurrian 1sg is -tta not -me. Some dialects show -me.

typological_matches: Agglutinative suffix stacking in fixed order
No verbal prefixes (matches Hurrian except neg. un-)
section_5_hurrian_comparison.conclusion: LA morphology shows strongest typological affinity with Hurrian among compared languages. Two suffix mappings (SE, JA) are strong; two more (NA, TE) are moderate. The exclusively suffixing, agglutinative profile is a strong typological match.

-JA <-> Greek -ia.note: Both mark ethnics/place-derivatives. Likely areal not genetic.

section_6_greek_comparison.conclusion: LA morphology is NOT Greek. No case endings map to Greek. Only -ja shows a structural parallel with Greek -ia, but this is areal (Aegean sprachbund). LA lacks Greek features: augments, reduplication for perfect tense, preposition-prefixes.

summary.key_finding: Linear A is an exclusively suffixing agglutinative language with at least 8 productive suffixes that stack in fixed order (up to 3 deep). Strongest external parallels are with Hurrian morphology.
